title: Understanding VA Math
desc: How combined ratings actually work -- with real examples, the bilateral factor, and key benefit thresholds

sections.heading: :brain: Why 50% + 30% Doesn't Equal 80%
sections.body:
If you thought VA ratings were simple addition, welcome to the most frustrating math in the federal government. **50% + 30% = 70%** in VA math -- not 80%.

> [!important] VA uses a **"whole person" theory** codified in `38 CFR 4.25`. Instead of adding percentages, each rating is applied to the **remaining "healthy" portion** of your body.

---

Think of it this way:
- You start at **100% healthy**
- A 50% rating means you're now at **50% efficiency**
- The next rating applies to that **remaining 50%**, not to the full 100%

The Combined Ratings Table in `38 CFR 4.25` spells out the exact math. The regulation states: *"the efficiency of the individual as affected first by the most disabling condition, then by the less disabling condition, then by other less disabling conditions, if any, in the order of severity."*

> [!warning] This is why the order matters -- you **always start with the highest rating**. The sequence changes the intermediate math even though the final combined value is the same per the table.

> [!tip] Understanding VA math helps you make strategic decisions about which claims to file next. Sometimes a 10% increase on an existing rating is worth more than a brand new 10% claim.

sections.heading: :chart: The Formula: Step by Step
sections.body:
Here's the actual calculation VA uses. Start with your highest rating and apply each subsequent rating to the remaining healthy percentage.

> [!check] Follow along with this example: **Two disabilities at 50% and 30%**

---

| Step | Action | Result |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Start at 100% efficiency | 100% healthy |
| 2 | Apply 50% rating | 50% disabled, **50% remaining** |
| 3 | Apply 30% to remaining 50% | 50 x 0.30 = **15% additional** |
| 4 | Total disability | 50 + 15 = **65%** |
| 5 | Round to nearest 10 | **70% combined** |

So **50% + 30% = 70% combined** -- not 80%.

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 4.25(a)`, combined values ending in **5 are always adjusted upward**. A value of 65% rounds to 70%, but 64% rounds down to 60%. That single percentage point can mean hundreds of dollars per month.

> [!tip] You can verify any combined rating using the Combined Ratings Table in `38 CFR 4.25`. Find your first rating on the left column, your second rating across the top, and the intersection gives you the combined value before rounding.

sections.heading: :chart: Worked Example: Three Ratings
sections.body:
Let's run a more complex scenario. **Three service-connected disabilities rated at 60%, 40%, and 20%.**

| Step | Action | Disability | Remaining |
|------|--------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Start | 0% | 100% |
| 2 | Apply 60% | 60% | 40% |
| 3 | Apply 40% to 40% remaining | 60 + 16 = 76% | 24% |
| 4 | Apply 20% to 24% remaining | 76 + 4.8 = **80.8%** | 19.2% |
| 5 | Round to nearest 10 | **80% combined** | -- |

The combined ratings table in `38 CFR 4.25` confirms: 60 combined with 40 = 76, then 76 combined with 20 = 81, which rounds to **80%**.

> [!warning] Notice the **diminishing returns**. That 20% disability only added about 5 points to the combined rating. This is why pursuing **higher individual ratings** often matters more than adding small new claims.

> [!tip] If you're at 76% combined before that 20% rating, you're already rounding to 80%. Adding the 20% doesn't actually change your final combined rating in this case. **Always calculate before you file** to understand the real impact.

> [!check] Use an online VA combined rating calculator to model different scenarios before deciding what to file next. Know your numbers.

sections.heading: :chart: Worked Example: Four Ratings
sections.body:
Here's where diminishing returns really bite. **Four disabilities rated at 50%, 30%, 20%, and 10%.**

| Step | Action | Running Total | Remaining |
|------|--------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | Apply 50% | 50% | 50% |
| 2 | Apply 30% to 50% | 50 + 15 = 65% | 35% |
| 3 | Apply 20% to 35% | 65 + 7 = 72% | 28% |
| 4 | Apply 10% to 28% | 72 + 2.8 = **74.8%** | 25.2% |
| 5 | Round to nearest 10 | **70% combined** | -- |

> [!warning] Four separate ratings totaling 110% in "normal math" only produce **70% combined** in VA math. The diminishing returns are brutal at the higher end.

> [!important] Per `38 CFR 4.25(b)`, "the conversion to the nearest degree divisible by 10 will be done only once per rating decision, will follow the combining of all disabilities, and will be the last procedure." Rounding only happens **once, at the very end**.

> [!tip] In this example, if that 10% rating were instead a 20% (maybe through an increase claim), the total would be 72 + 5.6 = 77.6%, which rounds to **80%** -- a full tier higher. Sometimes increasing an existing rating beats filing new claims.

sections.heading: :star: The Bilateral Factor (38 CFR 4.26)
sections.body:
Here's a hidden boost most veterans don't know about. If you have disabilities affecting **both arms, both legs, or paired extremities**, VA adds a **10% bilateral factor** before combining with other disabilities.

> [!important] The bilateral factor is automatic -- VA should apply it whenever you have qualifying paired disabilities. But mistakes happen. Know the math so you can verify.

---

**How it works:**
1. **Combine** the bilateral disabilities first
2. **Add** 10% of that combined value (not combine -- ADD)
3. Treat that result as a **single disability** and combine with remaining ratings

**Example: 20% right knee + 10% left knee + 50% PTSD**

| Step | Action | Result |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Combine bilateral knees | 20 + 10 = 28% (from table) |
| 2 | Add bilateral factor | 28 x 0.10 = 2.8, so 28 + 2.8 = **30.8** (round to 31 for combining) |
| 3 | Combine 50% with 31% | **66%** |
| 4 | Round to nearest 10 | **70% combined** |

> [!tip] Per `38 CFR 4.26(d)`, if including the bilateral factor actually produces a **lower result** than not including it, VA must use whichever calculation is **more favorable to you**. The bilateral factor can only help, never hurt.

> [!check] If you have any paired extremity conditions (both knees, both shoulders, both ankles), make sure VA is applying the bilateral factor. Check your rating codesheet -- it should show the bilateral factor calculation.

sections.heading: :brain: Rounding Rules
sections.body:
Rounding is where VA math gets really high-stakes. A single percentage point can mean the difference between rating tiers -- and hundreds of dollars per month.

> [!important] Rounding only happens **once** -- at the very end after ALL combining is done.

---

**The rules:**

| Combined Value Ends In | Rounds To | Example |
|-----------------------|-----------|--------|
| **5 through 9** | UP to next 10 | 65% becomes **70%** |
| **1 through 4** | DOWN to previous 10 | 64% becomes **60%** |
| **0.5** | UP per VA interpretation | 74.5% becomes **75%**, then **80%** |

> [!danger] The difference between a combined **74.9% and 75.0%** is the difference between a **70% and 80% combined rating**. That can be **$400+ per month** in compensation. Know exactly where your combined rating falls before rounding.

> [!check] Understanding rounding helps you decide which claims to prioritize. If you're sitting at 73% combined, you need claims that push you past 75%. If you're at 76%, you're already rounding to 80% -- focus on getting past 85% next.

sections.heading: :money: Key Rating Thresholds and What They Unlock
sections.body:
Certain combined rating levels are game-changers that trigger additional benefits beyond just higher monthly compensation. Know these thresholds.

---

| Rating | What It Unlocks |
|--------|----------------|
| **30%** | **Dependent allowances** (spouse, children, dependent parents). Below 30%, no dependent add-ons. |
| **50%** | Higher compensation with dependent allowances. Some **state benefits** trigger here. |
| **70%** | Meets one schedular threshold for **TDIU** under `38 CFR 4.16(a)` (if one disability is 40%+) |
| **100%** | Full schedular rating. **Chapter 35 DEA**, **CHAMPVA**, **property tax exemptions**, and more. |
| **100% + SMC** | **Special Monthly Compensation** at housebound rate (SMC-S) if single disability at 100% plus additional at 60%+ |

> [!important] The jump from 20% to 30% is massive -- it unlocks **dependent allowances** that can add hundreds per month for a veteran with a spouse and children. If you're at 20%, getting to 30% should be priority #1.

> [!check] **TDIU** under `38 CFR 4.16(a)` requires either one disability at **60%+** OR a combined rating of **70%+ with one disability at 40%+**. If you can't work due to service-connected conditions, TDIU pays at the **100% rate** even without a 100% schedular rating.

> [!tip] At 100%, your dependents may qualify for **Chapter 35 DEA** (up to 36 months of education benefits) and **CHAMPVA** healthcare. These benefits alone can be worth tens of thousands of dollars. Also check your state for property tax exemptions -- many states offer full exemptions at 100%.

sections.heading: :fire: Strategic Implications
sections.body:
Now that you understand the math, use it. Every filing decision should be informed by where you stand and what gets you to the next threshold.

> [!important] Because of VA math, each additional rating gives you **diminishing returns**. A veteran at 80% who adds a 10% disability only goes from 80% to 82% -- which still rounds to **80%**. That same veteran getting a 30% increased to 50% could push from 80% to 86% -- rounding to **90%**.

---

**Strategic priority order:**
1. Get your **highest-rated conditions rated as high as possible** first
2. Pursue **increase claims** on conditions rated below what the evidence supports
3. Don't ignore small ratings -- they can push you **past a rounding threshold**
4. Identify **secondary conditions** under `38 CFR 3.310` that stack bilateral factor benefits
5. If you're close to 100%, calculate whether **TDIU** under `38 CFR 4.16` gets you there faster

> [!check] Before filing anything new, run your current combined rating through a VA calculator and model what each potential claim would do to your combined. **File the claim that moves the needle most.**

> [!quote] A veteran at 82% combined (rounds to 80%) files for tinnitus at 10%. New combined: 83.8% -- still rounds to 80%. Zero net effect. That same veteran files an increase on their 30% knee to 40%. New combined: 86% -- rounds to 90%. That's the power of strategic filing.

> [!tip] Secondary conditions are often the fastest path to higher combined ratings. A service-connected back condition causing radiculopathy in both legs? That's two new ratings PLUS the bilateral factor. Model it out.
